coredata.dc:title: Molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica embedded with carbon dots and semiconductor quantum dots as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for diniconazole
coredata.dc:creator: Amjadi, Mohammad
Jalili, Roghayeh
coredata.dc:description: A dual-emission mesoporous structured molecularly imprinted sensor was designed for specific recognition and sensitive ratiometric detection of diniconazole ‏ (DNZ). In this probe, a carbon dot-doped silica core serves as a reference and provides a built-in correction for environmental effects. CdTe/CdS quantum dots (QDs) are encapsulated in the pores of the mesoporous silica and provide analytical signal. The fluorescence of CdTe/CdS QDs is selectively quenched in the presence of DNZ‏, accompanied by a visual green-to-blue color switch. The sensor has a linear response in the range of 20–160µgL−1 with a detection limit of 6.4µgL−1 and excellent recognition specificity for DNZ over its analogs. The applicability of the developed method for analysis of real samples was evaluated through the determination of DNZ in soil, river water and wastewater samples and satisfactory recoveries were obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymer
Quantum dots
C-dots
Diniconazole
Mesoporous silica
Ratiometric sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CdTe/CdS QDs and CDs
Spectral characteristics and structure of mMIP@CDs/QDs
Optimization of conditions for DNZ detection
Analytical performance of the ratiometric probe
Reproducibility and stability of the sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectrum of mMIP@CDs/QDs under a single wavelength excitation at 350nm.
(a) TEM and (b) SEM image of mMIP@CDs/QDs.

(a). Fluorescence spectra of the mMIP@CD/QD ratiometric probe upon the exposure to different concentrations of DNZ; (b) corresponding calibration graph (Error bars represent the standard deviation for three measurements) (c) color changes of the dual emission mMIP@CD/QD upon the addition of DNZ under a UV lamp. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence response of mMIP@CD/QD and mNIP@CD/QD for DNZ and its structural analogs. (Concentration of DNZ, DFZ, PNZ, TBZ, and HXZ were 100µgL−1). Inset: effect of TBZ on the binding of DNZ to mMIP@CD/QD. (Error bars represent the standard deviation for three measurements).

Schematic illustration of the architecture of the mMIP@CDs/QDs probe and the working principle for the detection of DNZ. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Results for the determination of DNZ in real sample.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The concentration unit for wastewater and water samples is µg L−1 and for soil samples is mg Kg−1.

head.ce:title: Molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica embedded with carbon dots and semiconductor quantum dots as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for diniconazole

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-emission mesoporous structured molecularly imprinted sensor was designed for specific recognition and sensitive ratiometric detection of diniconazole ‏ (DNZ). In this probe, a carbon dot-doped silica core serves as a reference and provides a built-in correction for environmental effects. CdTe/CdS quantum dots (QDs) are encapsulated in the pores of the mesoporous silica and provide analytical signal. The fluorescence of CdTe/CdS QDs is selectively quenched in the presence of DNZ‏, accompanied by a visual green-to-blue color switch. The sensor has a linear response in the range of 20–160µgL−1 with a detection limit of 6.4µgL−1 and excellent recognition specificity for DNZ over its analogs. The applicability of the developed method for analysis of real samples was evaluated through the determination of DNZ in soil, river water and wastewater samples and satisfactory recoveries were obtained.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular imprinting technique is now a common and effective strategy for production of polymeric artificial receptors for the specific molecular recognition (Bompart et al., 2015; Beyazit et al., 2016). Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have been widely used in different fields such as sensors (Wackerlig and Lieberzeit, 2015), drug delivery (Kempe et al., 2015), catalysis (Mirata and Resmini, 2010) and solid phase extraction (Nestora et al., 2016).

The unique optical properties of quantum dots (QDs) including high photostability and photoluminescence efficiency, sharp emission profile and size-dependent emission wavelengths make them appealing as fluorophores in optical sensing (Resch-Genger et al., 2008). Nevertheless, selectivity is still a challenging concern for QD-based sensors. Surface modification of QDs by using a molecular imprinting technique paved a suitable approach to overcome this problem.

Carbon dots (CDs) which are the newest member of the quantum dot series, have recently attracted considerable attention because of their excellent properties such as water solubility, biocompatibility, simple synthesis route, high photo- and chemical stability and tunable excitation and emission spectra. CDs are considered to be the next generation green nanomaterials to replace conventionally used fluorophores such as organic dyes and conjugated polymer (Zhang et al., 2015a; Zhang and Yu, 2015b).

Currently, most of the reported MIP-modified QD sensors are single wavelength intensity modulation type probes (Huy et al., 2014; Zhang and Chen, 2016; Zhang et al., 2015a, 2015c). These sensors are usually affected by some factors, such as concentration change and inhomogeneous distribution of sensors, instrument efficiency, and environmental conditions of the complex samples, which prevent the precise quantitative determination. In contrast, ratiometric measurement, which involves the simultaneous measurement of fluorescence signals at two or more well-resolved wavelengths followed by calculation of their intensity ratio, has been devised to circumvent these unfavorable effects (Wu et al., 2015, 2016; Zhu et al., 2012; Song et al., 2009). Up to now few papers have been reported on the development of ratiometric MIP-based sensors (Xu and Lu, 2015a, 2015b).

Triazole fungicides are systemic pesticides widely used to control fungus diseases in field crops. Despite their extensive usage, these compounds not only disturb endocrine activity in humans, but also have various harmful effects on mammalian biological systems because of their mutagenicity and carcinogenic properties (Wang et al., 2014a, 2014b; Bordagaray et al., 2014). Diniconazole (DNZ, Fig. S1a, Electronic Supplementary Material, ESM) is a highly active triazole fungicide and plant growth regulator. Because of its extensive usage, environmental problems have arisen from its residue (Wang et al., 2014a; Yang et al., 2006). Bioaccumulation of DNZ in non-target soil and aquatic organisms has been reported, which causes negative impacts on them (Wang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2014a). Considering these facts, developing fast, simple, and sensitive methods to monitor DNZ in various environmental samples is of great importance.

Herein, we report on a molecularly imprinted mesoporous structured probe for ratiometric fluorescence detection of DNZ ( Scheme 1). This probe possesses two emission peaks at 440 and 530nm under a single excitation wavelength of 350nm. The green emitting CdTe/CdS QDs are encapsulated into the pores of mesoporous silica to serve as the response signal label, and the blue-emitting CDs, embedded in silica core, act as the reference signal. The high pore volume and nano-sized pores of mesoporous silica provide high sensitivity and fast response time.

All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used as-received without further purification. 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), trisodium citrate, tellurium (reagent powder, 99.999%), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 99%) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, 99%) and cadmium chloride (CdCl2·2H2O, 99.9%) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (US). Diniconazole, Difenoconazol, Tebuconazole, Penconazole, and Hexaconazole were kindly provided by GYAH Co. (Karaj, Iran). DNZ stock solution with concentration of 100µgL−l was prepared in methanol and stored at 4°C. Working solutions were prepared daily by further dilution of the stock solution with water. Britton-Robinson buffer was prepared by mixing phosphoric acid (0.1M), acetic acid (0.1M) and boric acid (0.1M). The pH of buffer solutions was adjusted by adding the necessary amount of NaOH. Ultra-pure water obtained from aquaMAX - Ultra 370 water purification system (Young Lin instrument Co., Korea) was used for all experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Perkin Elmer LS45 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a quartz cell (1cm×1cm). The slit widths of excitation and emission monochromators were both set at 10nm. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by a Cary-100 spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia). All optical measurements were carried out under ambient temperature. The size and structure of mMIP@CDs/QDs were characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Mira 3 FEG, Tescan Co., Czech Republic). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurement was carried out with a Tensor-27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Co., Germany). Brunauer–Emmett– Teller (BET) surface area was determined by nitrogen adsorption/ desorption at 77K using a Quantachrom C Instrument. (Chem BET-3000, Quantachrom C., USA).

A gas chromatograph (GC-2014, Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with a splitless/split injector and flame ionization detector was employed for DNZ determination. GC conditions were as follows: The sample were separated on a SPB-1 capillary column (100% dimethyl siloxane, 30m×0.25mm i.d., and film thickness 0.25µm) (Supelco, Bellefonte, USA), with the following oven temperature programming: initial temperature 90°C (held 2min), ramped at 20°C min−1 until 220°C, then at 10–290°Cmin−1 and held at 290°C for 3min. The FID temperature was maintained at 300°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CdTe/CdS QDs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CdTe/CdS QDs and silane functionalized CDs were synthesized as described in previous works (Zhenyu et al., 2008; Jalili and Amjadi, 2015). Details are given in Electronic Supplementary Materials (ESM).

mMIP@CD/QD nanoparticles were prepared by a sol-gel based method. In a typical procedure, 10mL of ethanol and 25mL of ultrapure water were added into a 250-mL round-bottom flask. Under vigorous stirring, 100μL of CDs and 100μL of ammonia (25%) were injected to the solution. Then, 100μL of TEOS and 20mL of ethanol were added drop wise with a constant-pressure dropping funnel, and the resultant mixture was stirred for 8h. 3.0mL of CdTe/CdS QDs, 40μL of APTES and 30mg of DNZ were dispersed in 30mL methanol and injected to the reaction system and stirred for 20min. Subsequently, 1.5mL of CTAB (0.2M), 0.2mL of NaOH (0.2M) and 100μL of TEOS were added to the above mixture and kept stirring for 12h in darkness at room temperature. The product was collected by centrifugation and washed with water and methanol several times with the aid of sonication to remove DNZ and CTAB. The removal of DNZ was checked by measuring UV absorption of supernatant solution. The obtained mMIP@CDs/QDs were re-dispersed in water and stored at 4°C. Non-imprinted particles (mNIP@CD/QD) as a control were prepared identically, except that the template (DNZ) was omitted.

To a 4.0-mL standard cuvette, 200μL of stock solution of mMIP@CDs/QDs, 100μL of Britton-Robinson buffer (pH=8, 0.12M), and an appropriate amount of DNZ standard or sample solution were added and the volume was reached to 3.5mL with deionized water. After incubation for 5min, the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 530 and 440nm (F 530/F 440) was measured using the excitation wavelength of 350nm.

Two soil samples, one from University campus (Soil 1) and another from a farm near Urmia city (Iran) were collected. Soil samples were spiked with DNZ standard and then 1.0g of each dried and well-homogenized sample was mixed with 10mL of acetone in glass vials. The sample was shaken for 1h and suspension was centrifuged for 10min at 4000rpm. The supernatant were stored in the dark at 4°C for use. Immediately prior to analysis, 2.0mL of the acetone extracts was transferred to glass test tubes, and the acetone was evaporated by a brief treatment with a stream of nitrogen followed by dissolution in 1.0mL of methanol.

A wastewater sample from an industrial unit producing the selected pesticide (Karaj, Iran) and a river water sample from Zola River (Urmia) were collected and stored in a polyethylene bottle in the refrigerator until analysis. The samples were filtrated through a filter paper (ALBET, DP 135 125, Barcelona, Spain) and analyzed without further pretreatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral characteristics and structure of mMIP@CDs/QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The embedding process was carried out by sequential addition of CDs and QDs so that they are separated in the different spaces of the nanoprobe. This prevents the potential energy or electron transfer between them. As shown in Scheme 1, CDs are coated by a silica layer via Stober method to obtain CD-embedded silica nanoparticles. The silica layer prevents direct contact between CDs and DNZ, thus providing a reliable reference signal for the ratiometric detection. Then the imprinted mesoporous silica shell is prepared on the silica layer of the CDs by the co-condensation reaction between TEOS and APTES (functional monomers) in the presence of pore-generating template (CTAB), analyte template (DNZ) and CdTe/CdS QDs. Subsequently, CTAB and analyte template are removed and mMIP@CD/QD particles are obtained. CTAB surfactant molecules are used as the template for high yield generation of mesoporous structure in silica shell.

Fig. 1 shows the fluorescence emission spectrum mMIP@CD/QD ratiometric probe. It exhibits well resolved dual emission bands at 530 and 440nm under a single excitation wavelength of 350nm. Fig. S2 (ESM) shows the fluorescence spectra of three different batches of mMIP@CD/QDs, which confirm the reproducibility of synthesis procedure.

The morphological structure of mMIP@CDs/QDs was characterized by SEM and TEM techniques. From TEM image ( Fig. 2a), we can see that mMIP@CDs/QDs are highly dispersive with average diameter of about 100±10nm. SEM image (Fig. 2b) shows the uniform spherical for particles. The rough surface and mesoporous nature of mMIP@CDs/QDs is also evident from this picture.

The mesoporous nature of the mMIP@CDs/QDs was further proved by BET analysis. The specific surface area of mMIP@CDs/QDs was found to be 249.1m2/g, which is much higher than that of the corresponding non- mesoporous structured MIP@CDs/QDs (22.9m2/g).

In order to achieve the highest fluorescence response for DNZ, the polymerization conditions such as loading quantity of QDs and amounts of TEOS and APTES should be optimized. As seen in Table S1 (ESM) the probes obtained by using 100μL of TEOS have relatively better fluorescence response to DNZ. The doping ratio of QDs and CDs is important for the ratiometric detection, so MIPs with different doping ratios were prepared. The results indicate that both the linear range and detection limit vary with variation of the CdTe/CdS QD proportion. When QDs are in a large proportion, the linear range is limited because the strong intensity of QDs masks the emission of CDs (reference peak). On the other hand, too much amount of CDs also narrows the linear range because of low intensity of CdTe/CdS QDs. The probe has the highest sensitivity when 3–4mL of QDs and 100μL of CDs are used.

The effect of APTES amount on the sensitivity of probe was also investigated within the range of 20–100μL. As shown in Fig. S3 (ESM), the largest fluorescence change is obtained with 40μL of APTES. Small amounts of monomer cannot react adequately with the template and would not create enough recognition sites, while excess monomer may cause homogeneous self-condensation and leads to the formation of fewer recognition sites (Yilmaz et al., 1999).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions for DNZ detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain a high sensitivity for the DNZ detection, some assay conditions which may affect the fluorescence quenching efficiency, such as the response time, pH value, buffer system, and amount of probe were optimized using quenching ratio defined as F530/F440 (or FQDs/FCDs).

The fluorescence of the mMIP@CDs/QDs at different pH values was measured. The intensity ratio (F530/F440) in the absence of DNZ was not affected by pH changes in the range of 6.0–9.0, while the intensity ratio in the presence of DNZ (100 µgL−1) was changed. Since the highest fluorescence quenching (Table S2, ESM) was observed at pH 8, this pH was selected for further experiments and Britton-Robinson buffer was used to adjust the pH.

The binding kinetics of template to mMIP@CD/QD was then studied by examining their fluorescence quenching by DNZ over time. The reaction reached equilibrium within 5min and the fluorescence intensity remained constant after this time (Fig. S4, ESM). As far as we are aware, non-mesoporous surface imprinted QDs normally need 10–60min to reach adsorption equilibrium (Chen et al., 2012; Sadeghi et al., 2016; Chantada-Vázquez et al., 2016). The fast adsorption dynamics of mMIP@CD/QD is attributed to the generation of recognition sites between the pores of the mesoporous silica which offered high accessibility of template molecules.

The effect of amount of mMIP@CD/QD was also studied by changing their concentration from 10 to 60mgL−1. The highest quenching efficiency was obtained when the concentration of mMIP@CD/QDs was 20mgL−1 (Fig. S5, ESM). Hence, the concentration of mMIP@CD/QD was fixed at 20mgL−1 throughout the work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the ratiometric probe
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3, the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe/CdS QDs at 530nm gradually decreases upon addition of DNZ whereas the blue fluorescence of CDs remains constant. As the intensity ratio of two peaks decreases, the fluorescence color of the probe solution changes from green to blue. The ratio of the fluorescence intensity is closely related to the amount of DNZ ( Fig. 4b). There is a good linear relationship (y=0.0078±0.00013x+0.067±0.0154, R=0.9979) between Δ(F530/F440) [i.e. (F530/F44)0–(F530/F440), where (F530/F440)0 and (F530/F440) are the intensity ratios in the absence and presence of analyte, respectively] and the concentrations of DNZ in the range of 20–160µgL−1 with a 6.4µgL−1 detection limit (signal-to-noise ratio of 3). Continuous color changes from green to blue upon addition of DNZ can be easily observed by the bare eyes under a UV lamp.

The dose response of ratiometric mNIPs to DNZ was also examined. From Fig. S6 (ESM), we can see that fluorescence intensity of mNIPs is also quenched by DNZ, but, the sensitivity is much lower and the linear range is narrower (40–150 µgL−1 with a detection limit of 12.3µgL−1). In these conditions, the imprinting factor is 2.2, which illustrates the success of the imprinting process. This also indicates that the recognition of DNZ by the probe is essentially an imprint-based phenomenon not a simple adsorption-based effect.

With regard to fluorescence quenching mechanism by DNZ, several possible pathways can be considered including energy transfer from QD to DNZ, electron transfer from DNZ to QD, hole transfer from QD to DNZ and quenching due to the decrease in the number of the emitting centers of QDs (Zhang et al., 2009). The fluorescence quenching cannot be due to energy transfer because of the absence of overlap between the UV absorption band of DNZ and the fluorescence emission band of the CdTe/CdS QDs. Electron transfer from DNZ to QDs is also not possible because at the chosen excitation wavelength, DNZ molecules are not excited. Therefore, other two mechanisms might be responsible for quenching. Amine-containing compounds have been reported to act as effective acceptors for photogenerated holes in the QDs leading to quenching of their fluorescence (Sharma et al., 2003). Moreover, such compounds may attach to the surface of QDs and remove the emitting states near the surface resulting in a significant fluorescence quenching (Zhang et al., 2009).

Comparison of the proposed method with some previously published analytical methods for DNZ shows that our method has promising potential for simple, quick and reliable detection of DNZ. The detection limit of our method is on the same order as ELISA (Jiang et al., 2011) and chromatographic assays (Farajzadeh et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2012). When compared to ELISA, our method does not need expensive antibodies or highly skilled personnel. Chromatographic analyses, on the other hand, need time consuming and complex pre-separation procedures and expensive and sophisticated instrumentation. Although the lower detection limit (0.027µgL−1) for DNZ was achieved by using dual-labeled time-resolved fluoroimmunoassay method (Sheng et al., 2016), it needs expensive biological and biomimetic materials and elaborated and time consuming procedures.

To demonstrate the role of mesoporosity, a non-mesoporous probe was also prepared by the same procedure in the absence of CTAB. The linear range, LOD and response time of this probe for DNZ were 200–450µgL−1, 40µgL−1 and 20min, respectively. It is obvious that the sensitivity of non-mesoporous MIP@CDs/QD is markedly lower and the response is slower than the mesoporous one. In fact, the mesoporous structure, as expected, increases the accessibility of binding sites to target molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In the optimized conditions, the fluorescence stability of mMIP@CDs/QDs was estimated by the repeated measurement of the fluorescence intensity ratio (F530/F440) of the probe every 10min. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for 6 measurements within 50min was 2.4%, which indicates the stability of the fluorescence of mMIP@CDs/QDs. Besides, when the products are stored at 4°C it is stable for more than three weeks. The intensity was found to be 94% of its initial value after three weeks.

Reusability is another important property for sensors. To investigate reusability of the mMIP@CDs/QDs, removal–rebinding cycle was repeated four times using the same probe. After each measurement, the mMIP@CDs/QDs were washed with methanol/water (1:1, v/v) to remove the analyte molecules. The RSD for four repeated analyses was 4.1%, which demonstrates the good reproducibility and physical robustness and mechanical durability of imprinted network of mMIP@CDs/QDs.

The selectivity of mMIP@CD/QD sensor was studied by comparing the fluorescence response with respect to some functional and structural analogs such as Difenoconazol (DFZ), Tebuconazole (TBZ), Penconazole (PNZ), and Hexaconazole (HXZ) (Fig. S1, ESM). The results are graphically shown in Fig. 4. The selectivity factors, calculated as the ratio of the quenching constant of DNZ to that of analogs, were 83, 39, 4.3 and 3.0 for DFZ, PNZ, TBZ and HXZ, respectively. As can be seen, the sensor has large selectivity factors for DFZ and PNZ and they have little influence on the fluorescence of MIPs, because they do not have functional groups capable of forming hydrogen bonds with the functional monomers of MIP. TBZ and HXZ contain hydroxyl groups that are similar to the template molecule, but cause smaller fluorescence quenching because the recognition sites of DNZ imprinted cavities are not complementary to them. On the other hand, mNIP@CD/QD does not show a significant selectivity for DNZ with respect to these analogs.

To investigate the resistance of MIP@CD/QD to interference from analogs, the competitive binding experiment was also performed by fixing the concentration of DNZ and increasing the concentration of TBZ. As shown in Fig. 4 (inset), the quenching amount displays no significant change with the increase of the CTBZ/CDNZ ratio.

Finally, the effect of some potentially interfering ions was studied by preparing solutions of DNZ (100µgL−1) in the presence of excess amounts of interfering ions. The tolerable mass ratios for relative error of 5% were over 1000-fold for Na+, K+, NH4 +, Ag+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cl−, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, F−, NO3 − (Fig. S7, ESM).

The mMIP@CD/QD ratiometric probe was applied for the determination of DNZ in soil, water and wastewater samples. Recovery studies were carried out by spiking the samples with various amounts of DNZ. A summary of the calculated mean DNZ concentration for each sample is shown in Table 1. The obtained recoveries range from 95.6% to 105.5%, which indicate the usefulness of the mMIP@CD/QD for the determination of DNZ in real samples. The accuracy of DNZ determination was further examined by comparing the obtained results with those of GC analysis. Statistical analysis of results by using Student's t-test indicates that there are no significant differences between the results of two methods.

In summary, by preparing mesoporous molecularly imprinted polymer containing both CDs and QDs, we have developed a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for specific recognition and sensitive detection of DNZ. This is a new strategy for pesticide residue detection with its own advantages and limitations. The mMIP@CD/QD sensor combines the high selectivity of molecular imprinting technique with high robustness of ratiometric measurement. Moreover, mesoporous structure of MIPs effectively solves the problem of low accessibility of binding sites in conventional MIPs. The proposed sensor was applied to the determination of DNZ in water, wastewater and soil samples with satisfactory results. However, further works can be carried out to explore the potential use of this strategy for detection of other pesticides and for analysis of other real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to thank Prof. M.A. Farajzadeh for kind helps in GC analyses and providing DNZ standard.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.04.045.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrochim. Acta - Part A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
